title: Thermostat settings: the cheapest tool in the house
description: The thermostat costs nothing and controls the biggest bill. The right temperature, timing it to your life, using radiator valves, and the on-low-all-day myth.
blurb: The thermostat costs nothing and controls the biggest bill. Used well it saves real money; used out of habit it quietly overspends.
lede: The thermostat is the cheapest energy-saving tool in the house, because it costs nothing to use and controls the biggest bill you have. Used well it saves real money; used out of habit it quietly overspends, heating empty rooms and sleeping houses to daytime warmth. This guide covers the right temperature to set, how to time the heating to your day, using the room thermostat and radiator valves together, and the truth about leaving the heating on low all day.
short_answer: Set the thermostat to the lowest temperature that is genuinely comfortable, often somewhere in the high teens to around 20C for a living room, and turn it down a degree to save without really noticing. Time the heating to come on shortly before you get up and home, and drop back when you are out or asleep. Use radiator valves to turn down rooms you do not use. Despite the myth, leaving the heating on low all day costs more for almost everyone, not less.

sections.h2: The right temperature: every degree counts
sections.html:
Every degree on the thermostat counts. Dropping the temperature by one degree trims a noticeable slice off a heating bill, commonly £60 to £100 a year, and is barely perceptible day to day, especially with a jumper on. The reason is that a warmer house loses heat to the outside faster, so it takes more energy to hold a higher temperature against the cold outside.

A living room is comfortable for most people somewhere in the high teens to around 20C. Bedrooms and hallways can be cooler. Set the thermostat to the lowest temperature that is genuinely comfortable, not the highest you can afford. There is no fixed correct number, because comfort depends on draughts, humidity, how active you are and what you are wearing, but pushing the setting up out of habit is where a lot of money leaks away.

If parts of the house feel cold at a sensible thermostat setting, the answer is usually to stop the heat escaping rather than crank the dial. Draught-proofing, heavier curtains and loft insulation let you feel warm at a lower setting. Our guide to what temperature should I set my thermostat goes into the comfort side.

There is no sense heating an empty house or a sleeping one to daytime warmth. Use the programmer or a smart thermostat to bring the heating up shortly before you get up and home, and let it drop back when you are out or in bed. This timing, matching the heat to when you are actually there to feel it, is where a lot of the saving hides.

A simple, effective pattern for a typical working household is two heating periods: a warm-up before you wake, off through the working day, then back on before you return and off again overnight, with the temperature falling back rather than to zero so the house never gets stone cold. Set the warm-up to start half an hour or so before you need the room warm, since the house takes time to reach temperature.

A smart thermostat handles this without you thinking about it, and the better ones learn how long your house takes to warm up and can hold off heating an empty house using your phone's location. They are not essential, though: a basic programmer set thoughtfully captures most of the benefit for nothing.

sections.h2: Room thermostat and radiator valves together
sections.html:
The room thermostat controls the whole system; thermostatic radiator valves control each room. Used together they let you heat where you live rather than the whole house equally.

Set the main thermostat for your main living space, the room you spend the most time in, and turn the radiator valves down in rooms you rarely use, like a spare bedroom. The boiler then has less radiator surface to feed in those rooms, so it burns less gas and the rooms you use reach temperature faster. This is zoning on a budget, and the trade-offs are set out in is it cheaper to heat one room or the whole house.

One caution: do not turn unused rooms stone cold all winter. A radiator valve set to frost protection or a low number keeps the room ticking over, which avoids condensation and mould on cold outside walls. Putting that right costs far more than the heating you saved, so keep unused rooms gently warm rather than off. The broader picture of one-room versus whole-house heating is in is it cheaper to heat one room or the whole house.

sections.h2: On low all day, or off when out?
sections.html:
A persistent myth says it is cheaper to leave the heating on low all day than to heat the house only when you need it. For almost everyone it is not. A well-timed system that heats the house when occupied and lets it cool when empty uses less than one trickling away around the clock.

The logic is simple. A house loses heat at a rate that depends on the difference between inside and outside temperature. Let the house cool while you are out, and that gap shrinks, so it loses less heat during those hours. Heat it back up when you return, and you have spent less in total than holding it warm all day. Heat a house you are in, not one you are not.

The one genuine exception is a very well-insulated home, where the temperature barely drops over a few hours anyway, so there is little to gain from letting it cool. Even then, leaving it on low is at best no cheaper, not actually cheaper. Our full piece on leaving the heating on all day works through the numbers, and it is one of the most common energy saving myths.

None of these moves costs anything beyond an optional smart thermostat, yet together they take a real bite out of the largest bill in the house. Here is the rough scale at current gas prices for a typical three-bed home.

HabitCostTypical annual saving
Turn thermostat down 1CFree£60 to £100
Time heating to occupancyFree£50 to £150
Zone with radiator valves£10 to £20 per valve£30 to £80
Stop leaving it on low all dayFree£50 to £120

At 7.33p per kWh gas, Ofgem cap July to September 2026. Savings are typical ranges and depend on your house, insulation and how cold the winter is. Worked examples; check your own bills.

Because the thermostat governs the heating, and heating is the biggest energy cost in most homes, getting these habits right is the highest-value free change you can make. Do them before spending on kit. The wider order of attack is set out in the heating overview, and for a seasonal check-up see the winter energy checklist.

faq.q: What temperature should I set my thermostat to?
faq.a: Set it to the lowest temperature that is genuinely comfortable, which for most people is somewhere in the high teens to around 20C for a living room, with bedrooms cooler. There is no single correct number, because comfort depends on draughts, humidity and what you are wearing, but turning it down a degree usually saves money without you really noticing.

faq.q: How much does turning the thermostat down by one degree save?
faq.a: Commonly around £60 to £100 a year for a typical gas-heated home, because the house loses heat to the outside more slowly when held a degree cooler. It is one of the cheapest savings there is, costing nothing and barely noticeable day to day, especially with a jumper on.

faq.q: Is it cheaper to leave the heating on low all day?
faq.a: For almost everyone, no. A well-timed system that heats the house when you are home and lets it cool when you are out uses less gas than one running low all day, because a cooler house loses heat more slowly while you are away. The only real exception is a very well-insulated home where the temperature barely drops anyway.

faq.q: Should I use the room thermostat or radiator valves?
faq.a: Both, together. The room thermostat controls the whole system, so set it for your main living space. The radiator valves control each room, so turn them down in rooms you rarely use. This way you heat where you live rather than warming spare bedrooms to the same temperature.

faq.q: Do I need a smart thermostat to save money?
faq.a: No. A basic programmer set thoughtfully, so the heating comes on before you wake and return and drops back when you are out or asleep, captures most of the saving for nothing. A smart thermostat adds convenience and can learn your home's warm-up time and use your location, but it is an optional extra rather than essential.
